header.title: Research Interests
header.subtitle: We combine machine learning with multiregional theories of decision-making and working memory. Our research focuses on understanding how computations necessary to solve complex tasks can be distributed across segregated brain regions through data-driven approaches and biophysical modeling.

hiringAlert.title: Open Positions
hiringAlert.content: If you are interested in computational neuroscience, machine learning, and working at the intersection of theory and experiments, please contact me with your CV and research interests. Check our publications to understand the type of research we do.

cards.title: Extracting Dynamical Systems from Large-Scale Recordings

sections.title: Motivation
sections.content: Neural computations supporting complex behaviors involve multiple brain regions, and large-scale recordings from animals engaged in complex tasks are increasingly common. A current challenge in analysing these data is to identify which part of the information contained within a brain region is shared with others. For instance, using linear decoding, one might find that a given area encodes all task-related variables but using decoding alone it is hard to identify which variables are actually communicated to a specific downstream area. This is particularly challenging when considering more than two interconnected areas.

sections.title: Approach & Findings
sections.content: To address this limitation, we train multi-region recurrent neural networks (RNN) models to reproduce the dynamics of large-scale recordings. This recordings can in principle be of different modalities (single units, fMRI, M/EEG, etc). For instance, we showcase this approach by reproducing the dynamics of more than 6000 neurons across 7 cortical areas from monkeys engaged in a two-dimensional (color and motion direction) context-dependent decision-making task. After fitting, we partition the activity of each area, separating recurrent inputs from those originating in other areas. Decoding analyses show that all areas encode both stimuli (color and direction), but selectively project different dimensions of their activity. Sensory areas (V4, MT and IT) project only one variable (color or direction) while compressing others, irrespective of the context or downstream area. In contrast, we observed that the prefrontal cortex (PFC) and frontal eye fields (FEF) projected different aspects of the stimulus, depending on the downstream area or context. In the model, PFC/FEF strongly compress the irrelevant stimulus dimension in their projections to fronto-parietal areas but not as much towards sensory areas. These preliminary results motivate a novel approach to study how different regions coordinate their activity to solve context-dependent tasks.

sections.title: Related Publications
sections.content: • Estimating flexible across-area communication with neurally-constrained RNN (CCN, 2024)

cards.title: Distributed mechanisms of working memory and decision making

sections.title: Motivation
sections.content: Working memory and context-dependent decision making are two complementary windows into how the brain supports flexible cognition. Working memory requires information to be maintained and updated over time, whereas context-dependent decisions require relevant information to be selected and transformed according to current goals. Our work studies these functions together to understand how distributed neural circuits coordinate memory, selection, and action across multiple brain regions.

sections.title: Working memory
sections.content: We study the neural basis of working memory using biophysical modeling and data analysis of monkey prefrontal cortex, human EEG, and TMS. This work addresses the long-standing debate over whether working memory depends on sustained activity, short-term synaptic plasticity, or both. Our results support a distributed view in which persistent and activity-silent mechanisms coexist and interact, with implications for cognition in health and in disorders such as schizophrenia and anti-NMDAR encephalitis.

sections.title: Decision making
sections.content: We study context-dependent decision making using multi-region recurrent neural networks and large-scale neural recordings from rats and monkeys. By fitting flexible models to population activity and reverse engineering the learned dynamics, we test how computations are distributed across segregated brain regions. This approach reveals how different cortical areas selectively represent and route task-relevant information while compressing irrelevant variables depending on context and downstream targets.

sections.title: Related Publications
sections.content:
• Pinging the brain with visual impulses reveals electrically active, not activity-silent working memories (PLoS Biology, 2021)
  • Interplay between persistent activity and activity-silent dynamics in prefrontal cortex underlies serial biases in working memory (Nature Neuroscience, 2020)
  • Synaptic basis of reduced serial dependence in anti-NMDAR encephalitis and schizophrenia (Nature Communications, 2020)
  • Early selection of task-relevant features through population gating (Nature Communications, 2023)
  • Task-Relevant Information is Distributed Across the Cortex, but the Past is State-Dependent and Restricted to Frontal Regions (CCN, 2025)

sections.title: Motivation
sections.content: Mental health disorders are characterized by alterations in cognitive function, but the neural mechanisms underlying these impairments remain poorly understood. Working memory is impaired in major neurological dysfunctions, including schizophrenia and anti-NMDAR encephalitis. Computational psychiatry aims to bridge this gap by using mathematical models and quantitative approaches to understand psychiatric conditions. By combining EEG recordings with biophysical models, we can uncover the mechanistic basis of cognitive deficits in affected populations.

sections.title: Approach
sections.content: We use EEG and biophysical models to understand the basis of impairments in cognition in affected populations. Our approach combines high-density EEG recordings from clinical populations with computational models that can capture both the underlying neural dynamics and behavior. By comparing model predictions with observed neural activity patterns, we can identify specific neural mechanisms that are disrupted in psychiatric conditions and propose targeted therapeutic interventions.

sections.title: Related Publications
sections.content:
• Synaptic basis of reduced serial dependence in anti-NMDAR encephalitis and schizophrenia (Nature Communications, 2020)
  • Neural signatures of reduced serial dependence in anti-NMDAR encephalitis and schizophrenia (PsyArxiv, 2024)
  • Towards biologically constrained attractor models of schizophrenia (Current Opinion in Neurobiology, 2021)

cards.title: Normative Modelling with Recurrent Neural Networks

sections.title: Motivation
sections.content: Traditional approaches to understanding brain function often start with the neural data and try to infer the computation. Normative modelling takes the opposite approach: we start with the computational goal and ask what neural implementation would be optimal. By training recurrent neural networks (RNNs) to perform cognitive tasks under biologically-inspired constraints, we can generate testable predictions about how the brain might solve these problems.

sections.title: Approach
sections.content: We develop and apply normative theories using task-optimized RNNs that incorporate biological constraints such as Dale's law, sparse connectivity, and metabolic costs. By systematically varying these constraints and comparing the resulting network solutions to neural data, we can identify which computational principles are fundamental to brain function and which implementation details vary across individuals or brain regions.

sections.title: Key Questions
sections.content:
• What are the optimal neural solutions for cognitive tasks under biological constraints?
  • How do different constraints (e.g., energy, connectivity, noise) shape neural computation?
  • Can normative models predict individual differences in neural dynamics and behavior?
  • How do normative solutions change during development and learning?

sections.title: Related Publications
sections.content:
• Early selection of task-relevant features through population gating (Nature Communications, 2023)
  • NeuroGym: An open resource for developing and sharing neuroscience tasks (PsyArxiv, 2022)

sections.title: MOTIVATION
sections.content: Neuromodulators — dopamine, norepinephrine, serotonin, and acetylcholine — are released broadly across the brain and are known to regulate arousal, motivation, learning, and cognitive flexibility. Despite their central role in cognition and their implication in psychiatric disorders, how neuromodulatory systems shape the low-dimensional dynamics of cortical and subcortical circuits remains poorly understood. A key challenge is linking the moment-to-moment fluctuations of neuromodulator release to the computations performed by large neural populations.

sections.title: APPROACH & FINDINGS
sections.content: Large-scale population activity and neuromodulator release across multiple brain regions are recorded simultaneously using electrophysiology and fiber photometry, through a collaboration with Nikolas Karalis. We then fit neuromodulated low-rank RNN models that implement distinct synaptic mechanisms — presynaptic, postsynaptic, and additive — and compare them using fixed-point and bifurcation analyses. Applied to the norepinephrine-PFC system, this approach reveals that at median NE release, PFC operates near a line-attractor regime enabling persistent memory-like dynamics, a result that does not generalize to other recorded regions such as BLA and thalamus. Mean-field analysis identifies the circuit mechanism as NE-driven regulation of gain in a specific subpopulation of excitatory neurons.

sections.title: KEY QUESTIONS
sections.content:
• How do different neuromodulators shape the dimensionality and stability of population dynamics across brain regions?
  • Are line-attractor dynamics a general signature of neuromodulation, or specific to particular region-modulator pairs?
  • How do neuromodulatory systems interact with task demands and behavioral state to reconfigure circuit computations?
